(21:52 GMT) Aurizon Raises FY Dividend Estimate; Will Keep Network Business

By Rhiannon Hoyle

Australian rail-freight operator Aurizon Holdings on Monday raised its annual dividend guidance as it reported an increase in first-half profit, and said it has decided to keep the whole of its network business following a review.

The company reported a statutory net profit of 235 million Australian dollars (US$166 million) for the six months to Dec. 31, up from A$233 million a year earlier. Underlying profit, stripping out significant items, rose to A$237 million from A$205 million, Aurizon said.

Directors declared an interim dividend of 12.5 Australian cents a share, up from 9.2 Australian cents a year ago. The company now expects to pay a full-year dividend of between 22-23 Australian cents per share, up from an earlier estimate of 19-20 Australian cents per share.

Aurizon has decided to retain its existing above- and below-rail integrated model following a review of the current structure, said Chief Executive Andrew Harding.

"The review, supported by external advisers, assessed a broad range of whole-of-business and minority structure options for Network including monetization and demerger scenarios," he said.

"We determined that retaining 100% ownership of Network remained the option that best delivers long-term value for our shareholders."

Write to Rhiannon Hoyle at rhiannon.hoyle@wsj.com

(21:53 GMT) *Correct: Aurizon 1H Underlying Profit A$237 Million, Up 16% On-Year

data.description:
(21:20 GMT) *Treasury Wine 1H Statutory Net Loss A$649.4 Mln
(21:21 GMT) *Treasury Wine 1H Profit Pre-Material Items, SGARA A$128.5 Mln, Down 46%
(21:22 GMT) *Treasury Wine 1H Earnings Before Interest, Tax and Other Items A$236.4 Mln, Down 40%
(21:22 GMT) *Treasury Wine 1H Revenue A$1.3 Bln, Down 17%
(21:23 GMT) *Treasury Wine Suspends Interim Dividend
(21:24 GMT) *Treasury Wine: Suspension of Dividend is Temporary Measure to Prioritize Capital Preservation, Reduce Leverage
(21:25 GMT) *Treasury Wine: Resumption of Dividends Will be Subject to Financial Performance
(21:25 GMT) *Treasury Wine: Underlying Performance of Key Brands Remains Positive
(21:26 GMT) *Treasury Wine: Expects 2H Ebits to be Higher Than 1H
(21:27 GMT) *Treasury Wine: 2H Ebits To be Driven by Improved Momentum in California Following Completion of Distributor Transition
(21:47 GMT) Treasury Wine Suspends Dividend After Large Half-Year Loss

By Mike Cherney

SYDNEY--Australia-based Treasury Wine Estates suspended its dividend after posting a large half-year loss, reflecting the vintner's struggles as it battled tough market conditions in the U.S. and China.

Treasury, which owns the iconic Australian brand Penfolds and DAOU and Frank Family Vineyards in the U.S., said its net loss for the half year ended in December was 649.4 million Australian dollars (US$458.3 million), reflecting a previously announced write-down on its U.S. assets. That compares to a A$220.9 million profit in the same period the prior year.

Treasury said its key earnings metric, known as Ebits, was A$236.4 million, down 40%.

The company said it wouldn't pay an interim dividend. It characterized the suspension of dividends as a temporary measure aimed at preserving capital and reducing leverage, and said the resumption of dividends would be dependent on financial performance.

Treasury posted earnings declines in all its key divisions, with Penfolds down 20%, its Americas unit down 64% and its Treasury Collective unit down 51%.

Looking ahead, Treasury said it expected its fiscal second half Ebits to be higher than the first half, driven by improved momentum in California following completion of a distributor transition.

The Australia-based vintner has had a rough few months, as it battled uncertain demand in China, a flagging wine market in the U.S. and a change to its California distribution. It scrapped its initial annual guidance and said it would write down the value of its U.S. assets. It also paused and later canceled a share buyback, said it would reduce customer inventory holdings in the U.S. and China, and launched a cost-cutting effort.

One big headwind for the Treasury has been China's attempt to rein in excess among its civil servants, which has hit demand for wine. In the U.S., luxury wine market trends have continued to moderate and the premium wine segment remains in decline, Treasury has said.

Treasury had shifted toward higher-margin luxury products amid waning global appetite for budget brands, but some analysts have expressed concerns that the write-down on its U.S. assets indicated that it overpaid for acquisitions.

There has been some momentum in recent days, however. Treasury said last week that it would slightly beat its prior guidance for the fiscal first half, and that it had settled a dispute with one of its U.S. distributors--though that involved Treasury repurchasing inventory held by the distributor.

Despite a bump last week, the vintner's shares have been on a downward slide. Shares closed Friday at A$5.24, down 33% since August and 53% below where they were at the end of 2024.

Write to Mike Cherney at mike.cherney@wsj.com

data.title: Treasury Wine's first-half profit halves, interim dividend suspended
data.description:
Australia's Treasury Wine Estates' first-half underlying profit nearly halved on Monday, and it temporarily suspended the payment of its fiscal 2026 interim dividend as a measure to prioritise preservation of capital.

The result underscores a reset in its two largest markets after 2025 shipments ran ahead of underlying demand, leaving elevated distributor inventories in China and the U.S. that management has moved to unwind.

The firm reported a 46.3% slump in net profit after tax before material items and SGARA of A$128.5 million ($90.85 million) for the six months ended December 31, down from A$239.6 million a year ago.

That slightly beat the Visible Alpha consensus estimate of A$127.8 million.

Adding to concerns, one of Treasury Wine's U.S. distributors shut down in California, forcing a scramble for new partners and leading to a 28.4% drop in first-half net sales revenue at Treasury Americas to A$283 million.

($1 = 1.4144 Australian dollars)

data.title: Australian Stocks on Course for Opening Rise — Market Talk
data.description: Australian equities look set to resume their recent rise at the start of the trading week. Local stock futures are up by 0.6% ahead of Monday's session, suggesting the S&P/ASX 200 is on course for an opening bounce. The benchmark index fell 1.4% Friday but still completed its best week since May 2025, rising 2.4% across five sessions. Ahead of the open, real-estate group Stockland reported a 19% rise in first-half profit. Bendigo & Adelaide Bank posted a 6.4% rise in first-half profit, while PPE manufacturer Ansell raised its dividend. U.S. equities provided a mixed lead at the end of last week. The DJIA added 0.1%, the S&P 500 finished less than 0.1%, and the Nasdaq Composite slipped 0.2%. (stuart.condie@wsj.com)

data.title: Australia's BlueScope posts two-fold jump in first-half earnings
data.description:
Australia's BlueScope on Monday reported a more than two-fold jump in its first-half earnings due to stronger U.S. spreads, higher volumes across key markets, and solid cost performance.

The steelmaker posted underlying net profit after tax of A$382 million ($270.07 million), compared to A$176.4 million last year.

($1 = 1.4144 Australian dollars)
